title: Add regression coverage for script-relative `.rules` bootstrap behavior
summary: Add focused regression coverage for the 0.5.1 bootstrap fix so `.rules` generation remains reliable when `zed-agentops-init` is invoked from arbitrary working directories. This ticket should verify that rule asset lookup no longer depends on the caller’s current directory and that both packaged-style and source-tree execution paths continue to create `.rules` correctly.
outputs: Regression tests covering script-relative rules lookup in `zed-agentops-init.sh`
Coverage for invocation from a working directory unrelated to the repository layout
acceptance_criteria: Test coverage proves that `.rules` creation succeeds when the init entrypoint is executed from a current working directory that does not contain `src/agentops_mcp_server`.
Tests verify that the bootstrap path resolves packaged rule assets relative to the script location rather than the caller’s working directory.
Source-tree execution remains covered so the fix does not regress repository-local use of `src/agentops_mcp_server/zed-agentops-init.sh`.
Regression checks fail if the bootstrap implementation reintroduces `${PWD}`-based rule source discovery for packaged assets.
Existing initialization expectations for `.agent`, `.gitignore`, and `.zed` scaffolding remain intact under the tested invocation paths.
tasks: Add tests that model execution from an arbitrary current working directory and assert that target workspaces still receive `.rules`.
Add assertions that cover script-relative rule asset resolution behavior in the bootstrap implementation.
Preserve or extend existing source-tree tests so local repository execution remains supported alongside installed-style execution.
Ensure the regression suite protects against future changes that accidentally depend on the caller’s working directory layout.
Keep the added coverage focused on `.rules` bootstrap reliability without weakening existing initialization checks.
notes: This ticket depends on the path-resolution implementation being completed first so tests can target the intended steady-state behavior.
The goal is regression protection, not a redesign of the bootstrap API.
Coverage should be strong enough to catch packaging-path mistakes and working-directory assumptions introduced by future refactors.
